petitioner_background: The petitioner is an Epidemiologist seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. At the time of filing on January 15, 2003, he was working as a Research Associate in the Department of Epidemiology at the Johns Hopkins School of Public Health and as an Adjunct Investigator in the Laboratory of Epidemiology, Demography and Biometry at the National Institute on Aging, National Institutes of Health. His research focused on cardiovascular disease epidemiology, sleep-related diseases, and related topics through NIH-funded studies including the Atherosclerosis Risk in Communities Study, the Sleep Heart Health Study, and the Cardiovascular Health Study.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence showing that he received a Memorial Award from the Johns Hopkins University School of Hygiene and Public Health. His Ph.D. advisor stated that because of the petitioner's extraordinary academic accomplishments he was granted this 'highly competitive and prestigious' Memorial Award, granted to the most outstanding graduate students in the Department.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the Memorial Award reflective of departmental recognition rather than national or international recognition. CIS views academic awards from universities and learning institutions as local or institutional honors rather than internationally recognized awards because they are limited to the individual school or institution presenting the award. The AAO also noted the award was presented for 'academic accomplishments' rather than excellence in the epidemiology field, and that graduate study is not a field of endeavor but advanced training for future employment. The petitioner's field cannot be artificially restricted to exclude epidemiologists who had completed their graduate studies and did not compete for a departmental graduate student award.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On appeal, the petitioner submitted several articles about his work published in December 2003, with the published material relating entirely to an article in the January 7, 2004 issue of Stroke.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that all of this evidence came into existence subsequent to the petition's January 15, 2003 filing date. Per Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45, 49 (Comm. 1971), a petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing; a petition cannot be approved at a future date after the petitioner becomes eligible under a new set of facts. While the AAO agreed that the petitioner's published study received a significant level of media attention, new circumstances that did not exist as of the filing date cannot retroactively establish eligibility.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On appeal, the petitioner submitted e-mails dated October 13, 2003 and thereafter requesting that he review articles for publications such as the New England Journal of Medicine, Journal of Women's Health, International Journal of Obesity, Medical Science Monitor, NeuroImage, and Stroke.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found this evidence came into existence subsequent to the petition's filing date. Per Matter of Katigbak, subsequent developments in the petitioner's career cannot retroactively establish that he was already eligible for the classification sought as of the filing date.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted five letters in support of the petition. A professor in the Department of Epidemiology at Johns Hopkins described the petitioner's role in the NIH-funded Atherosclerosis Risk in Communities Study, his presentation at the Society for Epidemiology Research on risk factors for survival after myocardial infarction, his paper at an American Heart Association Conference on alcohol effect on subclinical cerebrovascular disease, contributions to papers on cardiac procedures trends and echocardiography, a coauthored paper on hormones and cardiovascular disease published in the American Journal of Epidemiology, and his work on sleep apnea and white matter disease in the brain stem (the first paper to show pathological abnormalities in the brain stem associated with impaired arousal ability), presented at an American Thoracic Society Conference. His Ph.D. advisor (now Professor of Public Health and Chair at University of Wisconsin Medical School) described his leading role in the Sleep Heart Health Study. An Assistant Professor of Medicine and Epidemiology at Columbia University described broadly recognized original scholarly contributions in cardiovascular disease epidemiology including papers on hormones and cardiovascular disease that 'proposed new directions.' Another letter discussed the paper 'A Hypothesized Relationship Between Relative Androgen Excess and Increased Cardiovascular Risk After Menopause' and 'Endogenous Postmenopausal Hormones and Carotid Atherosclerosis: A Case-Control Study of the Atherosclerosis Risk in Communities Cohort,' both published in the American Journal of Epidemiology. The Chief of the Geriatric Epidemiology Office at NIA/NIH described the petitioner's role in several NIH-funded studies and presentations at international scientific conferences.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that acclaim is generally not established by the mere act of presenting work at a scientific conference, and the record contained no documentation that presenting was unusual in the field or that invitations to present were extended only to top researchers. Participation in conferences is routine and expected in the medical research community. The AAO acknowledged the observation that the petitioner was among the first to show pathological changes in the brain stem may be a cause of impaired arousal ability, but found the petitioner had not provided adequate evidence that this finding was widely recognized throughout the field as a major contribution. Observations that the petitioner's work 'may greatly improve prevention' of life-threatening diseases or cites 'the potential' of his research are not adequate to establish that findings are already nationally or internationally acclaimed. The AAO noted that any journal article must offer new and useful information to be accepted, but it does not follow that every published scientist has made a major contribution. The AAO noted his witnesses consisted of his supervisor at NIH and four professors affiliated with Johns Hopkins, and letters from institutions where the petitioner studied and worked are not first-hand evidence of acclaim outside those institutions. The nature of a junior faculty position is not an indicator that petitioner is at the very top of the field. Without extensive documentation predating the filing date showing findings were unusually influential or highly acclaimed throughout the greater field, the work does not rise to the level of a contribution of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner initially submitted evidence of his authorship of two articles appearing in the American Journal of Epidemiology in 2001 and 2002 and a few scientific conference abstracts. On appeal, he submitted evidence of additional articles appearing in Stroke, the American Heart Journal, Neuroepidemiology, the American Journal of Hypertension, Sleep, Circulation, and the American Journal of Epidemiology, all published subsequent to the filing date. The appellate submission also included citation indices showing his 2001 and 2002 American Journal of Epidemiology articles were cited an aggregate total of 17 times.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that publication of scholarly articles is not presumptive evidence of sustained national or international acclaim; the greater scientific community's reaction must also be considered, with citation history being a more reliable gauge than mere publication. Articles published subsequent to January 15, 2003 cannot retroactively establish eligibility per Matter of Katigbak. Of the 17 citations to his 2001 and 2002 articles, only three existed as of the petition's filing date. While the citation indices demonstrated some measure of interest in his work, he had not shown that his publication record elevated him above almost all others in his field at the national or international level. The weight of his 2001 and 2002 journal articles was diminished by a lack of evidence they were widely influential or had earned him national acclaim as of the filing date.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding that virtually all of the evidence presented on appeal related to circumstances or events arising after the filing date, which cannot retroactively establish eligibility per Matter of Katigbak. The petitioner failed to demonstrate that he met at least three of the ten regulatory criteria required to establish sustained national or international acclaim. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner distinguished himself to such an extent that he achieved sustained national or international acclaim or placed within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence was not persuasive that his achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field at the national or international level. The burden of proof remained with the petitioner, who did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
